entities.description: The 1983 plain-text deposit titled The life of St. John Norbert, publicly supplied by Oxford and used as the input to U-M's 2023 revision.
entities.label: Oxford Text Archive deposit 0162
notes: Oxford metadata credits John Capgrave as creator and P. J. Lucas as contributor, while the record title says compiled by P. J. Lucas. Lucas's exact production role remains to be clarified.

The deposit uses # for thorn and $ for yogh. It is the complete publicly supplied 1983 file, but it is not textually complete against Smetana: U-M reports about 200 omitted lines and evident OCR errors.

access.accessMethod: Borrow through Internet Archive controlled digital lending if eligible and signed in.
access.entityLabel: Cyril Lawrence Smetana's 1977 edition of The Life of St. Norbert
access.format: Restricted lending scan; PDF/JP2 derivatives are not anonymously downloadable
notes: The item metadata marks access-restricted-item true. No encrypted or account-gated derivative was cached.

bibliographic.citation: John Capgrave, The Life of St. Norbert, ed. Cyril Lawrence Smetana, Studies and Texts 40 (Toronto: Pontifical Institute of Mediaeval Studies, 1977), viii + 179 pp.
bibliographic.title: The Life of St. Norbert

entities.label: Cyril Lawrence Smetana's 1977 edition of The Life of St. Norbert
notes: The edited text begins under the heading Life of Saint Norbert / MS HM 55 on p. 19 and runs through p. 155. The volume also contains introduction, comments by Peter J. Lucas, glossary, bibliography, scriptural citations, and index.
The title page and catalog establish Smetana as modern editor, despite the current XML's first AUTHOR element placing his name where a reader might mistake it for medieval authorship.

rights.basis: The visible copyright page states copyright 1977 by the Pontifical Institute of Mediaeval Studies. United States Copyright Office Circular 15A states a 95-year term for protected pre-1978 works and automatic renewal for 1964–1977 publications, placing ordinary 1977 protection through the end of 2072.
rights.component: Smetana's 1977 editorial text, introduction, apparatus, comments, glossary, and other original editorial matter
notes: The date range assumes the ordinary validity of the displayed copyright notice. Public-domain status of Capgrave's medieval text does not remove copyright from sufficiently original modern editorial contributions.

access.accessMethod: Use the Huntington collection page, HDL object, or library catalog record.
Apply as a Huntington reader, submit an Aeon request, and obtain required curatorial approval before an onsite consultation.
access.contact: The Huntington Library reading-room and curatorial staff via Aeon
notes: The item record says RESTRICTED USE: extended retrieval; curatorial approval required. Reader eligibility and approval are separate from image-order availability.

access.accessMethod: Create or use a Huntington Aeon account and submit a reproduction request for the complete physical object. Approval, feasibility, and final quotation depend on condition and restrictions.
access.contact: The Huntington Library Imaging Services through Aeon
access.format: Requested manuscript PDF scans or other quoted digital files
notes: The current price list states manuscript PDF scans at USD 1 per page for non-profit use and USD 2 per page for commercial use, with administrative charges for larger orders; fees can change and an exact quote controls.

A whole-object request should explicitly include both boards, spine, fore edge, pastedowns, every flyleaf and blank, ff. 1r–59v, the lifted former pastedown f. i recto-verso, color/scale targets, and any separate binding views. The corpus work should then be mapped narrowly to ff. 1r–59v.
Huntington states a standard turnaround of about 15 business days, with extended handling for requests over 50 images; HM 55's restriction may affect feasibility.

dateStatements.label: production
dateStatements.notes: The poem self-dates completion to Assumption week 1440, and the repository dates the manuscript [1440].

entities.description: A presentation copy of The Life of St. Norbert made by John Capgrave, written in England in 1440 and presented to John Wygenhale, abbot of West Dereham; Huntington describes the manuscript as evidently autograph. The work occupies ff. 1r–59v; a separate lifted former pastedown, f. i recto-verso, is a Latin breviary fragment and is not part of the work.

holding.notes: Physical consultation is restricted and requires curatorial approval and extended retrieval.

entities.label: Huntington Library mssHM 55
notes: Huntington calls the volume a presentation copy made by Capgrave and describes it as evidently autograph.

DIMEV identifies HM 55 as the unique witness of record-2973 and localizes its language to Norfolk through LALME/eLALME. That localization applies to the witness's language, not automatically to Capgrave's place of composition.

physicalDescription.extent: 59 folios for the Capgrave codex, plus separate former pastedown f. i recto-verso
physicalDescription.format: Codex in fifteenth-century leather over oak boards
notes: Capgrave wrote the work in secretary script, normally 35 verse lines per page, and supplied interlinear corrections in his own hand.
The opening historiated initial on f. 1r depicts Capgrave presenting the book to Abbot John Wygenhale; f. 59v ends with Capgrave's Feliciter signature and trefoil.
The reversed inner bifolium of quire 6 transposes the physical order of ff. 44 and 45.
The repository suggests production in England, possibly at the Augustinian house at Lynn; this is not treated as a secure place of composition.
physicalDescription.support: Parchment; separate lifted former pastedown fragment also preserved

rights.basis: The literary work and manuscript were created in 1440, centuries before modern United States copyright terms.
rights.component: John Capgrave's 1440 Middle English text and medieval manuscript content
notes: This determination does not apply automatically to Smetana's 1977 editing, later encodings, modern photography, or provider platform terms.

access.accessMethod: Open the Huntington Digital Library object or exact IIIF Presentation 2 manifest.

localCopies.label: Complete Huntington HM 55 published five-image selection
notes: ZIP contains the exact provider manifest, inventory, and all five published JPEGs requested at the effective 1800-pixel IIIF width profile.
Coverage is partial relative to physical HM 55: the unrepresented manuscript leaves, the separate breviary fragment, and most binding surfaces are unavailable in this object.

workPortion.label: Published selection images overlapping The Life of St. Norbert
locators: manuscript f. 1r / IIIF canvas 1 / bundle image 000001
manuscript ff. 32v–33r / IIIF canvas 2 / bundle image 000002
manuscript f. 59v / IIIF canvas 3 / bundle image 000003
notes: Canvases 4–5 show the fore edge and inside back cover rather than additional work text.

The original ContentDM canvas identifiers are retained inside the cached IIIF manifest and inventory as technical locators; the work-portion links use working HDL item pages because direct canvas requests returned 404 during review.

rights.basis: The IIIF manifest omits the license field and links no item-level open license. Huntington says no permission is required to quote or publish public-domain material or material whose copyright it does not own, requests precise citation, and leaves rights assessment to the researcher.
rights.component: Huntington digital manuscript photography and IIIF derivatives
notes: The public-domain medieval text and public download availability do not establish a Creative Commons or unrestricted commercial license for the modern image files.

entities.description: The complete provider-published five-canvas Digital Scriptorium selection: f. 1r, a spread showing ff. 32v–33r, f. 59v, the fore-edge binding, and the inside back cover. It is not a continuous or complete manuscript facsimile.
entities.label: Huntington HM 55 five-image published selection
notes: Canvases 1–3 overlap the work at f. 1r, ff. 32v–33r, and f. 59v. Canvases 4–5 document binding surfaces.
The official record explicitly describes these images as a partial scan made for Digital Scriptorium in the early 2000s.

rights.basis: The IIIF manifest omits the license field and links no item-level open license. Huntington says no permission is required to quote or publish public-domain material or material whose copyright it does not own, requests precise citation, and leaves rights assessment to the researcher.
rights.component: Huntington digital manuscript photography and IIIF derivatives
notes: The public-domain medieval text and public download availability do not establish a Creative Commons or unrestricted commercial license for the modern image files.

entities.description: The Digital Index of Middle English Verse record identifying Capgrave's poem, rhyme-royal form, unique HM 55 witness, and Smetana and Horstmann editions.
entities.label: DIMEV record-2973 for The Life of St. Norbert
notes: The release XML identifier is record-2973 and the live DIMEV record number is 2973; 1805 is the IMEV/NIMEV number and must not be substituted into recID, because recID=1805 resolves to a different poem.
The dataset describes the opening as Joy and grace peace love faith and charity, the seven-line ababbcc stanza, HM 55 ff. 1–59v, and the Feliciter signature on f. 59v.

bibliographic.title: Capgrave, Bokenham, and Late Medieval Saints' Lives

bibliographic.title: The Date of John Capgrave's Life of St Norbert

next_steps: Consult Smetana's full introduction and later Capgrave source studies for a recension-level identification.
Compare Capgrave's additions, omissions, and chapter sequence against the BHL 6248–6250 Vita tradition without assigning any one BHL number until direct evidence supports it.
If an exemplar is proposed, verify whether the claim names a physical witness, an abstract recension, or only a modern edition.
notes: The present manifest records a generic translation relationship in work metadata but does not create an unproved physical-source edge.
openQuestions.question: Which exact Latin Vita Sancti Norberti recension and physical exemplar did Capgrave translate?

next_steps: Read Gribbin's full appendix, the underlying Peter J. Lucas chronology argument, and subsequent responses rather than treating the publisher summary as the complete case.
Keep the secure Assumption-week 1440 completion separate from any proposed earlier compositional phase.
openQuestions.question: Was the poem wholly composed in 1440, or does the completed presentation text revise material begun before 1422?

next_steps: Ask the Oxford Text Archive for legacy accession documentation and production notes for ota:0162.
Compare the full OTA text against Smetana's pages and apparatus to enumerate omissions and transformations rather than relying only on the U-M summary.
Clarify whether the deposit's compiled by credit refers to OCR preparation, text correction, or another editorial contribution.
openQuestions.question: What exact OCR, keying, compilation, and checking workflow produced OTA 0162, and what was P. J. Lucas's role?

next_steps: Submit a Huntington Aeon reproduction request with an explicit whole-object shot list and ask whether complete existing master photography is available.

Ask whether HM 55's restricted physical status permits full imaging and whether a non-profit or commercial rate applies to this repository's intended research and possible downstream commercial editions.
On delivery, retain the complete lawful object in source-cache and map the work narrowly to ff. 1r–59v while keeping the Latin former pastedown fragment separate.
notes: Only the five-image Digital Scriptorium selection was publicly accessible on the review date.
openQuestions.question: Can a complete authorized color surrogate of physical HM 55 be obtained, including every textual leaf, the lifted former pastedown fragment, blanks, endleaves, and all binding surfaces?

next_steps: Seek written clarification from Oxford/U-M about the intended scope and licensable rights behind the item-level CC license, especially for commercial reuse.
Ask PIMS for permission or a scope clarification before reproducing or closely deriving protected Smetana editorial matter commercially.
Review the exact terms accompanying any Huntington reproduction delivery before sharing files or using images in publication.
Keep research use of source formatting distinct from copying protected editorial expression or redistributing modern facsimile files.
openQuestions.question: How do the OTA/U-M CC BY-NC-SA 3.0 licenses, Smetana's still-protected editorial contribution, and any Huntington delivery terms constrain commercial self-publishing derived from this source chain?

reviewNotes: The approved CME90162 PDF explicitly selects the recent_changes XML. The sibling CME_from_OTA file has a different wrapper and header but an identical EEBO textual subtree; both file fixities are preserved without treating the sibling as a separate text revision.

The five-canvas Huntington object is a complete capture of the provider's published selection but only a partial surrogate of physical manuscript HM 55. It must not be presented as a whole-codex facsimile.

The medieval text, Smetana's modern editorial contribution, OTA and U-M encodings, Huntington photography, and DIMEV dataset have separate rights records. No rights statement is inherited between layers.
Negative availability findings are dated 2026-07-14 and do not exclude private, mediated, or newly published reproductions.

lineage.summary: The publication-bound General Corpus source is the fully headed 2023 University of Michigan CME XML at CME/source/recent_changes/additions_2023/CME90162.xml, not the sibling legacy-wrapper copy under CME_from_OTA. Its encoded body revises Oxford Text Archive deposit 0162 by comparison with Cyril Lawrence Smetana's 1977 edition: U-M restored about 200 omitted lines, repaired evident OCR errors, and added structure and source locators. The OTA deposit was apparently captured from Smetana, whose edition transcribes Huntington Library mssHM 55, ff. 1r–59v, a presentation copy made by John Capgrave that Huntington describes as evidently autograph. Huntington publishes only five selected images, three of which show the work; the complete codex is not publicly digitized but a full reproduction may be requested through Aeon. DIMEV record 2973 (XML record-2973; IMEV/NIMEV 1805) independently identifies HM 55 as the sole known witness and cites the source edition.

catalogSummary.author: John Capgrave
catalogSummary.region: England; the surviving witness is linguistically localized to Norfolk, with possible production at Lynn and dedication to the abbot of West Dereham; Huntington describes the presentation manuscript as evidently autograph
catalogSummary.title: The Life of St. Norbert

catalogingSubject.label: The Life of St. Norbert
catalogingSubject.scope_note: John Capgrave's 4,109-line Middle English rhyme-royal life of Saint Norbert, completed in Assumption week 1440, including its dedicatory prologue and closing envoy. The cataloging subject is not Smetana's 1977 scholarly book, the 1983 OTA text, the 2023 U-M encoding, the repository XML, physical manuscript HM 55, or the unrelated Latin breviary fragment preserved as HM 55's lifted former pastedown.

contentParts.label: Modern U-M editorial frame

locators.value: BODY/HEAD[1–2], OPENER/BYLINE/DATELINE, and ARGUMENT/P before DIV1

notes: This prose is modern representation-level paratext, not part of Capgrave's medieval work or one of its 39 intrinsic verse sections.
contentParts.summary: Modern English project framing precedes the medieval poem: two headings identify the electronic edition and work, an opener supplies the project byline and February 2023 dateline, and a prose argument explains the OTA-derived text, U-M repairs, and structural markup.

contentParts.label: Dedicatory prologue
notes: The XML creates one DIV2 TYPE=prologue. item_count is zero because the work's 37 numbered items are its chapters; the prologue remains a separately counted verse section.
contentParts.summary: Capgrave addresses his patron, defends his verse-making, announces his translation of Norbert's life, links his own Augustinian community with Norbert's order, and offers the book for reception into the patron's fraternity.

contentParts.label: Life of Saint Norbert in 37 chapters

locators.value: L 71–4095; 37 DIV2 TYPE=chapter elements with N=1–21, xxii, 23–37

notes: The physical and printed end folio/page is shared with the envoy; the line boundary prevents the narrative part from claiming the whole terminal surface exclusively.
contentParts.summary: The numbered narrative follows Norbert from his worldly youth and sudden conversion through his religious foundations, preaching, church offices, conflicts, miracles, death, and posthumous signs. The analytical boundary ends immediately before the two-stanza envoy within chapter 37.

contentParts.label: Envoy to the book
notes: DIMEV treats the envoy as part of record-2973 and cross-refers a separate legacy description to that record. The analytical part does not imply a distinct independently transmitted work.
The envoy is not a separate XML division; it occupies the final fourteen lines of chapter 37.
contentParts.summary: Capgrave sends the little book to the abbot of West Dereham, names John Wygenhale, identifies himself as translator, and dates completion to Assumption week 1440 before the final prayer for heaven.

contentParts.label: Terminal formula Feliciter
notes: Although the XML numbers Feliciter as L 4110, scholarship reports 4,109 verse lines and the word is not treated as a verse line or stanza.
contentParts.summary: The single Latin word Feliciter follows the poem and is associated with Capgrave's signature and trefoil on f. 59v.

assertion.notes: Lines 4104–4108 explicitly name Capgrave, Assumption week, and the year 1440.

notes: The secure completion date does not resolve the earlier-phase proposal summarized by Gribbin: Peter J. Lucas argued that Capgrave may have begun the work before 1422 and completed and dedicated it in 1440.

dateStatements.display: 1440 presentation manuscript, described by Huntington as evidently autograph
notes: HM 55 is the 1440 presentation copy that Huntington describes as evidently autograph; DIMEV records it as the sole known witness. Composition and first attestation therefore coincide chronologically but remain distinct descriptive claims.

extent.description: The represented item contains one modern prose editorial frame outside the intrinsic work and 39 analytical verse sections: the prologue, 37 chapters, and envoy. The intrinsic verse consists of 4,109 consecutively numbered lines: a 70-line prologue, 4,025 narrative lines, and a 14-line envoy embedded at the end of chapter 37. At seven lines per rhyme-royal stanza, this is 587 stanzas. The XML has 4,110 L elements because it also assigns N=4110 to the non-verse Latin terminal formula Feliciter; it encodes no LG stanza elements. It has 38 DIV2 containers, since the editorial frame is not a DIV2 and the envoy remains inside chapter 37 rather than receiving its own division.

formStatements.description: A 4,109-line poem in 587 seven-line rhyme-royal stanzas (ababbcc), with a 70-line prologue, 37 numbered chapters, and a 14-line envoy. The final Latin Feliciter is outside the verse count.

genres.label: Religious translation
notes: The generic Latin Vita source is secure, while its exact recension and exemplar remain unresolved.
The cataloging subject is the medieval intellectual work; Smetana's edition, OTA deposit, U-M revision, repository artifact, manuscript, and selected images remain separately bound lineage entities.

The XML's AUTHOR fields are bibliographic header fields for the modern edition and repository, not reliable work-level authorship fields. Capgrave's authorship and translation are established by the poem, manuscript catalog, DIMEV, and scholarship.
The extent distinguishes 4,109 intrinsic verse lines from 4,110 XML L elements. Feliciter is retained as a terminal formula rather than silently discarded or counted as verse.

The XML has a duplicate PB 31 and no PB 32, plus a duplicate folio milestone 25v and no milestones for 16r, 30v, or 35v. These anomalies are documented as representation facts and are not silently normalized into source pagination or foliation.
The language facet remains Middle English. The single Latin terminal word and the separate Latin breviary fragment do not make the cataloged work bilingual.

Commercial and redistribution decisions must use the linked lineage rights records; public-domain status of the medieval work does not erase rights or terms attached to modern editing, encoding, or photography.

next_steps: Consult Smetana's full introduction and later source studies for explicit recension and exemplar claims.
Collate Capgrave's chapter sequence, additions, and omissions against the BHL 6248–6250 Vita tradition before assigning a specific BHL identifier.
Preserve any abstract recension and any proposed physical exemplar as distinct source layers.
openQuestions.question: Which exact Latin Vita Sancti Norberti recension and physical exemplar underlie Capgrave's translation?

next_steps: Read Peter J. Lucas's full chronology argument and subsequent scholarship rather than relying on preview summaries.
Model any substantiated early phase as a separate date assertion without weakening the explicit 1440 completion.
openQuestions.question: Does the secure Assumption-week 1440 completion encompass the whole composition, or did Capgrave begin a version before 1422?

next_steps: Review paleographical, dialectal, biographical, and institutional scholarship on Capgrave's location in 1440.
Keep Norfolk dialect localization, Lynn production proposal, and West Dereham patronage as separate assertions unless direct evidence links one to composition.
openQuestions.question: Can the work's place of composition be established more narrowly than England without conflating witness dialect, possible manuscript production at Lynn, and dedication to West Dereham?

assertion.notes: The statement is witness-specific despite the whole-work discovery scope.

notes: Do not convert the witness's Norfolk dialect localization into a secure place of composition.

scope.description: Language of the sole known witness HM 55, which Huntington describes as evidently autograph

assertion.notes: The repository says possibly the Augustinian house at Lynn.

notes: This is not cataloged as a confirmed place of composition.

scope.description: Possible production context of the presentation manuscript that Huntington describes as evidently autograph

notes: Capgrave sends the book to John Wygenhale, abbot of Derham near Stoke, identified by scholarship and the repository as West Dereham. This patronage location is not proof that the poem was composed there.

scope.description: Institutional destination and patronage context named in the dedication and envoy

notes: The source XML's top-level AUTHOR elements describe the modern edition and manuscript repository; they must not displace Capgrave's directly attested medieval authorship.
Capgrave says that he will translate the story and identifies himself as the friar who translated it. The exact Latin recension and exemplar remain unresolved.

scope.description: Middle English verse rendering of a Latin Vita Sancti Norberti, with Capgrave's prologue, envoy, additions, and omissions
Smetana's 1977 source edition of the medieval work, not authorship of the work itself

subjects.label: Norbert of Xanten, Saint, Archbishop of Magdeburg (d. 1134)
Religious orders and the Rule of Saint Augustine
notes: The prologue explicitly links Capgrave's Augustinian order and Norbert's followers under Augustine's rule.

summaries.value: John Capgrave's Middle English verse life narrates Norbert's conversion, religious vocation, foundation and governance of the Premonstratensian order, ecclesiastical career, miracles, and sanctity. Capgrave closely translates a Latin Vita while reshaping it selectively for a 4,109-line rhyme-royal poem addressed to John Wygenhale, abbot of West Dereham. A dedicatory prologue connects Capgrave's Augustinian community with Norbert's order, and a two-stanza envoy names the recipient, translator, and completion in Assumption week 1440.

DIMEV records Huntington HM 55 as the poem's sole known witness at ff. 1r–59v; Huntington describes it as a presentation copy made by Capgrave and evidently autograph. The codex also preserves a separately lifted former pastedown with a Latin breviary fragment; that fragment is physical context, not a content part or language facet of The Life of St. Norbert. The five public Huntington images are selected surfaces rather than a complete codex surrogate.

CME90162 represents only the medieval poem and a short project argument, not Smetana's full 1977 volume. U-M revised an incomplete OCR-like OTA capture against the edition, restoring about 200 omitted lines and adding chapter, line, page, and folio markup. The edition, encoding, and manuscript remain separate descriptive layers.

notes: The preferred modern English title follows Smetana and DIMEV; manuscript f. 1r is identified by its opening rather than treated as evidence for this exact editorial title form.
This is the heading in the 2023 U-M encoding, not necessarily a manuscript-supplied title.
This is the current Oxford repository title for OTA 0162. Its St. John Norbert wording is retained as a legacy access title, not adopted as the preferred identification.
titles.value: The life of St. John Norbert

notes: A cataloging uniform title carried in the U-M header; it does not identify the exact Latin recension or physical exemplar translated by Capgrave.

titles.value: Ioye, grace & pees, loue, feith & charite

notes: The following Latin Feliciter is modeled separately as a terminal formula rather than counted as a 4,110th verse line.
titles.value: Aftyr þis lyf, I pray God send us heuene!

work.summary: John Capgrave's Middle English verse life narrates Norbert's conversion, religious vocation, foundation and governance of the Premonstratensian order, ecclesiastical career, miracles, and sanctity. Capgrave closely translates a Latin Vita while reshaping it selectively for a 4,109-line rhyme-royal poem addressed to John Wygenhale, abbot of West Dereham. A dedicatory prologue connects Capgrave's Augustinian community with Norbert's order, and a two-stanza envoy names the recipient, translator, and completion in Assumption week 1440.
